speaker: interviewee
text: There was water in the-- bottled water.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took sink water.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had bottled water. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We buy it mostly when it's a sale. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a favorite brand?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] No, any kind of water. They're all good water.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We need to--

speaker: interviewee
text: It comes from the mountains, the Pennsylvania mountains.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once I was in, in France, in Rouen. And I took some water from the sink. It was delicious. So I kept drinking. And the people said, imagine someone from America can taste what good water we have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Okeydokey. You're going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's tape 3, Phyllis Dreazen talking with Allen Caine in Philadelphia. It's August-- no, it's October 23, 1995. November 6, 1942, when you went to the train, what kind of day was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened to be a nice day. Nature was always against us with them. We had heavy winters in those-- that year. My grandfather was from the 1800s. He never remembered heavy winters. Nature was against us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your grandfather come with you on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was always with them. I never took my eyes off of the-- those two people, and my, my uncle, and my two aunts. And we-- I sat between my grandfather and my grandmother. We were riding two days and one night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Second day, in the middle of the day, I see my father-- my grandfather. His lips was moving. I said, Grandpop, what are you-- what are you-- what prayers are you saying? Because I used to be, like, a copycat. I looked up to my grandfather. He was a smart man. What kind of prayers? I said? He said, I'm saying confession. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] I said, why? You say that one before you go to bed. He says, I don't think there is not much left-- is much left for me anymore. So he already said confession.

speaker: interviewee
text: A good person, in our religion, every night before he goes to bed, he says confession. Because he may-- you never know. He may not get up in the morning. And this way, he says confession if he's-- with his clear mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my grandfather was that person, too, every night. But here in the middle of the day, he says, we're already running around too long. And I want to have my conscience clear. I said, Grandpop, don't say like-- don't talk like that. And I start crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] That was the, the second day. We went through one night. The stench was terrible. People would just-- couldn't lay down or couldn't stand. The smell, everything was awful. How many people-- I don't know how many people were in the wagon, in one car. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Finally, we stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I-- we didn't have any food to take with us. So I did take with me-- I got ahold of a bag of sugar. I don't know how I got it. I, I, I used to like sweets. But in a time like that, I liked everything. So I had a little-- made out of a [NON-ENGLISH], a little bag hung-- hanging up here. And every time, put them-- ate the sugar.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stopped. The train started to-- the locomotive started to give a sound. I used to work here in a place in Philadelphia, US Steel, that just reminded me of Auschwitz-Birkenau. A train used to come into the plant where I worked. And they had wire, barbed wired, in that place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when that train used to signal the same thing, I used to shake. Finally, I quit that place. I got to have a steady place to work. And I couldn't take it. So that's what happened in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the signal?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the SS to come. We are here. They had their barracks, the SS. The driver from the locomotive was a Polish fellow-- Pole. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Finally--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- it was the train whistle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, but a special whistle. A special whistle.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it sound?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't describe it. But my body knows when-- I hear it here sometimes, too. That's what I-- I, I shake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it one blow on the whistle?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. That's a rough whistle. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't describe it-- how it sounds. And we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- how many toots did it make?

speaker: interviewee
text: It kept repeating till, till they came-- the SS came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a pattern of sound or just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was like, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like-- I can't-- I don't know. I can't describe it. It was-- it was awful. I mean, something like that is always in your mind. We were all sitting, waiting. We didn't know that we are arrived on a place that we-- that's the end of that. We thought maybe we'll wait till-- [SIGHS] maybe a train has to go by. And we're still going to continue. All of a sudden, we heard knocking on the doors, but rough. The door opened up. Everybody out! Don't take nothing with you. Everything stays inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what language?

speaker: interviewee
text: German, yes. As we came up, we saw-- right away, we saw guys coming in, cleaning up everything. They threw the stuff on the other side of the train, not the place where we go out. Funny-looking guys with stripes-- we didn't know what kind of people these are. And lights-- we had so much lights there. You could read a paper with small print.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this at night--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at night, yes. Pretty late at night. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And somebody hollered, women and children, separate! Old people together with the women and children! Able-bodied people, separate-- whoever can still work! OK. I didn't stop no place. I just looked around, you know, a kid. I'm nosy. I hear a commotion somewheres. I walk over. I see a little baby, laying on the ground, blood coming out of the baby's mouth. I knew that baby. We had a doctor in our town, a, a German Jew doctor. [SIGHS] So we start hollering to the lady-- to the mom from the baby, so-and-so, there is-- the baby's dying. Just leave it-- the baby alone. Don't bother it. Don't touch the baby. She gets closer to the baby. And she falls over the baby. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we're looking around. We don't see the doctor. We knew that it was his family. Finally-- he had one leg-- one wooden leg he had. He was in the First World War, injured fighting for Germany. Now, he's a-- he's a Jew now. And we started to holler to him, look what happened to your family! Just leave him alone, he spoke in German. He came over. He collapsed, too. What he did, he gave them all a pill.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cyanide.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cyanide. When you bite into that, that looks like blood. But they were dying one on top of the other. [SIGHS] I seen that, I got all shook up. I'm running back to my grandmom. I told them about it. My grandfather says, that's the end of us. Where he knew, how he know-- he was a smart man. It just came to him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I see my brothers already with the-- all the young guys on the side, vis-a-vis us. And I'm just turning around. I'm moving back. I'm standing in front of my grandmother. She chases me away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I look at her. Maybe it was an accident. I don't believe it. I said, Bubbe, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]? Why are you doing this to me? Why are you chasing me away?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] She says, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mein Kind, you go to your brothers [SOBBING] because we are going to die. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I said to my grandmom, remember, I told you, I want to help you, in case you're not going-- you, you-- we're getting resettled. You're not going to die. She says, no, honey, we're not. I say, either way, don't chase me away. I have a-- I had a mother. Well, she used to get all excited when she heard my, my mom's name. She says to me, listen, you go to your brothers. Your mother will look up. She'll look up. She'll look for you. She'll watch over you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I couldn't persuade her. But that time, a week later, I wished-- I, I, I was always saying, I wish to God that I, I would know what was going on here. I would never go away from those people. Because I suffered so much that I, I was wishing myself to dead-- to be dead instead. I lowered my head. I looked at the last time-- I looked at my little brothers-- my little brother, my little-- two little sisters. They all looked at me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were thinking, how come he's better than we are? I'm just saying what they were thinking. Pale faces-- they were, like-- they were with-- sick. I lowered my head.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to my brothers. They were just watching me. They didn't know what was going on. I came over to my-- [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SOBBING] I came to my older brother. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He took me in his arm. He put me in the front of him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were waiting. The rumors were going around. We were-- we are waiting for a, a doctor to come. In the meantime, we were standing and waiting. An SS officer came around to our group. He was looking for two strong men. He took him out. That guy-- these two guys were supposed to help the old people go up in the trucks-- the young kids, the old people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And one guy I knew very well, a friend of mine-- and I didn't want to look back. I didn't want to look at my grandparents no more. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they never came back, these two people. After they pushed them up, the SS ran over to push these two up, too. They went right with the old, old people. They were young guys in there, 18 years-- 17 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the mother of the children? Your father's wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was with the children. You never went-- got separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was with your grandparents also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, they stayed together in one little group-- the way everything was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you saw them go up on the truck, then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- I looked around. They were going already. I didn't know where they were going. I didn't believe that they were-- where they were going, but I knew that. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain what you mean by that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't believe that they were going to get gassed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- where did you hear the word 'gas'?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I didn't know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know where they went. But what I believed-- after these two kids didn't come back, I knew that something bad is going to happen to them. Otherwise, he would have-- [POINTS] But that's how they worked. That's how the Nazis worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm standing-- we're all standing and waiting. And in the meantime, I said to my brothers, I got some sugar. Would you like to have some? And I was very, very thirsty. I, I couldn't-- my throat was dry, from the sugar, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, after an hour or so, we hear a motorcycle. A motorcycle comes over, with-- three-wheeler. A big guy comes out. And later on, I found out his name was Kramer, Dr. Kramer. And right near the motorcycle, right in front, he puts a leg-- his leg up in there, [POINTS] you know, like this. He start [POINTING] motioning to people-- should come toward him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SPEAKING GERMAN], right, left, right, left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When it came to me, what do I do? I was a short guy. I was only-- this was the time where people grew. But they took away everything for, for me. Shall I stretch myself? My brother was in back of me. He was just holding me up. Otherwise, I, I was going to collapse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I heard the word 'right.' My brother just-- like, keep moving. Keep moving. You heard-- you said 'right.' And all my brothers went to the right. When I looked around, and I saw whoever he sends to the left-- sent to the left, I couldn't believe my eyes. We were two and a half days on the road. We didn't eat. We were hungry before we got on the-- on the road. We didn't eat. We didn't sleep. People didn't shave. OK, they look tired. They'll look old. If a person has a beard, he looks older. He put them to the left to be gassed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Finally, put them on the side, the one from the left. He made us-- turned around. And we start marching.

speaker: interviewee
text: We get-- I asked one, one officer-- I said, what, what town is that? He says, this is Auschwitz, which happened to be Birkenau. But Auschwitz is number I. Birkenau is number II. And that's how it works.

speaker: interviewee
text: We start to march. We get to the gate. We had-- I counted it-- 85 people. Music-- with that marching music they were playing, the best beautiful music-- we had to walk according to the music. And already a guy standing, counting us up. We went inside. They motioned us where to go in. As they were walking in, I saw a little river-- whatever it is. In the books that I have, there is still a-- the river is still on it. When I was there, there was no more river, last time I was there. And as thirsty I was, I jump out of the line. And I jump on my knees. I started to clean the water, to get the-- it looked like something fat on this-- on the top of it. You know, I wanted-- I picked it up like that. I wanted to drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hear a voice, a kapo. I turned around. A kapo, a criminal, he tells me in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. You dumb dolt, don't you know you're not allowed to drink that-- you can't drink that water? I said, why not? I asked a question which you're not allowed to ask. Later on, I found out you don't ask questions to, to these guys. He said, because they're throwing ashes in there. Ashes? I figured, well, ashes can be any ashes. I never believed what kind of ashes. I didn't ever-- heard of it. And he walked away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am working on the water. [SIGHS] [CRYING] As I looked in that water, it was unbelievable-- bones-- any kind of bones from bodies, heads, legs, hands. And the water was so thick, like, you could put a-- like a thick soup. Then I got sick. I got up. I went inside where the, the whole gang, my, my family and everybody else was. And I told my brothers what I saw. They just looked at me like, like a kid tells you a, a, a story, but he doesn't know what he's talking about. That, that-- it didn't go into their heads. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I got a haircut. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then the barber told me that, see, [NON-ENGLISH], that is the gas chamber here. That's where your, your folks are getting-- are now buried-- burnt-- not burnt, gassed. It was only a wall in-between us. And by the time we got there, they were probably dead already and already on the fire, burning. They didn't have the crematoriums to burn the people. They had the pits. They burned them in the pits. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Everybody was finished. That's all I could get saved-- was my shoes. And the rest, they put on a uniform on me. I looked like a typical criminal already.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the stripes?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the stripes. Everything was striped. They gave us little [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] socks that were striped. They made it themselves. You could see the edges and everything. They were striped. A a, cap, striped-- everything striped. Couldn't recognized each other. We went into a barrack. [SIGHS] Everybody, as many people we were, everybody cried. We are doomed now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next day, they took us out. They made us-- they taught us how to salute, take off the caps when we go to roll call. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And they were putting us down in line to get our numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five across?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, no, no, no, by alphabet. Mine was C. I stayed in C the same way. My brother was before. I was before my brother, just so-- they watched me.

speaker: interviewee
text: As it comes to me, he looks at me. He ignores me. He calls my brother over. I said, I'm next. Why don't you give me the number?

speaker: interviewee
text: He looks at me. He says, you're not getting a number. You can't even make a cake of soap. If you don't get a number-- everything went by the number to get you a little bit of soup-- if you call it soup-- your little bit of coffee in the morning. Yeah, everything has to be a number. He wouldn't give me the number. My brother got a-- all these brother got a number. And he pushes me back in there. I go over there. He says no, you're not getting the number.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started to cry. He said-- I said to him, I'm 18 years old. Why shouldn't you give me the number? He said, no, you're not.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then finally, they talked to each other. He says, well, let him go. But he'll be good to somebody's bed. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I come from a small town. I never heard of that before-- what was going on in there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, he gave me the number. And what is your number?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show it to us, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] One morning-- as a matter of fact, the second night in the morning, we go out to the barracks. Everybody gets a piece of bread. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Once you got out of the barrack, you were never allowed to see the-- till late at night, to, to go in. Doesn't matter if it's fires, flood, anything, you cannot go in. And they give you a little bit of some kind-- ersatz coffee. So half of it, you washed your face. And half of it, you dranked. And here I hear a commotion. A friend of mine, we went to cheder together. He walks out. And they accused him that he was inside already. He took-- that already, he took bread twice. You could never go back inside. But what they'd do, these guys used to steal from us. And then they accused you. You were taking twice. So they killed the guy. Well, anyway, the kid started crying. I wasn't inside any-- I wasn't-- the second time. I never did twice-- taken bread twice. Well, one hollers to the other. They're starting to beat that kid over the head till he fell in the mud. Mud wasn't that-- Birkenau had mud up to your knees. Sometimes, you left your shoes. You couldn't pull, pull your feet up. The shoes got lost in there. Till he fell into the mud. Once you fell into the mud-- they used to carry sticks with them. They put the stick on his neck. One guy stepped on one side, the other guy stepped on the other side. That's the first-- the first casualty I see, what they did to a person. Till that kid died, stick his tongue out, choked himself. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I remember it was a weekend. Every day used to come shipments with people-- every day, every day. We-- I was already assigned to a Barrack 8. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One morning, it started-- I didn't want to run into trouble. It was November. It was cold. Oh, it rained. Every day, it rained down there. We were always soaking wet.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm standing. They were asking, who is a tailor? So I figured to be out of trouble, I'll say, I'm a tailor. What did you have to do-- the numbers from your arm, you had to put on one here and one here-- no, the other way, one here, and one here. So they gave you a needle and everything. So [SIGHS] I'm sitting on the ground on one knee. And here's a line in front of me. And everybody-- I'm sewing this side, and so-and-so. Here, a big guy comes along. I look at him. He says to me-- he was from France. He says to me, [NON-ENGLISH], what's going on here? I said-- I said, what, what kind of work are you do? I asked him. He says, I'm a doctor. I said, you're a doctor? The first thing you do, you get rid of your stomach.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, he says, well, the way it looks, it'll come in time. I'll get rid of it. It'll get-- they'll get-- they'll get rid of it. And, you know, we, we talked. And that was it. He was a doctor. His name was Dr. Brill. Later on, I found out he was born in Romania. And he studied for a doctor in Paris, France-- wonderful gentleman. Wonderful gentleman. And [SIGHS] then that third day, we're standing at roll call. They took my brother out, the-- my-- Zelek, that number two brother. I didn't know what. Later on, I found out they took him, the Sonderkommando to burn people. I always saw them walking in line with pieces of timber for the fire. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the evening, we stay in roll call. We had to have-- after roll call, we used to get a little bit of soup. And there's a person missing. We're standing. We were standing for hours. Finally decided, started to-- some-- that's what they did. And it's already late at night. And we are standing. Dead or alive, they've got to have the person. Or the person could've got lost in the mud. That's how much mud we had. Or somebody threw him in the toilet, in the latrine. And they're looking for the dead-- for the body. Doesn't matter to them what. Otherwise, he says he's not going to sign the roll call. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So the two guys-- they are paying attention to the barrack. They're keeping it clean and everything. They had an idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a barrack just for dead bodies. They couldn't burn fast enough on the fire. They kept them in stack on the-- hundreds of them stacked up until under the ceiling-- bodies. There were two doors. And you keep pushing them forward through the one door, forward. They went into that barrack. They opened up one door, the back door. There is a guy already-- where, where the guy died-- or beaten to death, whatever. They undressed him naked. And they put with magic marker the number on his stomach. And he was laying in the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when he called the roll call, so many live ones and one dead, or two dead. We're all-- as he goes into the dead house, he sees already all the dead bodies. He, he said, I didn't know what I'm looking for. Here are dead bodies laying with the number already on it. And his lips are still moving.

speaker: interviewee
text: He figured he'll take him out. He's already dead, considered dead and gone and everything. Take him out-- they took him out. They washed off his magic marker. And they threw him against the wall. And the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the roll call already open-- comes around. He counts up and sees one dead. He signs it. The guy used to turn around. He put the sheet and signed it on the-- on the back of him. [SIGHS] And that's it. We gave the soup. But I found out they took the dead body back in--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --washed him off. The man-- the man is alive. It's all--

speaker: interviewer
text: Today?

speaker: interviewee
text: --they need. The dead man? He was alive. He was already laying in the dead-- in the dead house. They took him in. They washed him off. They dressed him. They got to get rid of him because there is one more guy in their block-- in their barrack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they gave him a little bit of soup, tea. They revived him.
